title: Diagnosis and Management of Endocrine Hypertension: Deciding Who to Screen for Genetic Causes
authors: William F. Young, Jr., MD, MSc.

items.title: Why elevated plasma normetanephrine often misleads clinicians
items.text: Clinicians frequently test for pheochromocytoma, but because of assay false positives and disease rarity, 97% of patients with elevated plasma normetanephrine do not have a tumor—driving costly downstream workup and occasional surgical misadventures.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "because of the false-positive rate of plasma normetanephrine and the rarity of pheochromocytoma, 97% of patients with elevated plasma normetanephrine do not have a pheochromocytoma."

items.title: How unenhanced CT attenuation can rule out pheochromocytoma before biochemistry
items.text: Among 376 histologically confirmed pheochromocytomas with unenhanced CT data, 374 had attenuation >10 HU (99.5%). An incidentaloma at 1.2 HU cannot be pheochromocytoma—skip biochemical testing and avoid false-positive metanephrine cascades.
items.reference: Case 1: "among the 376 pheochromocytomas for which unenhanced CT attenuation data were available, 374 had an attenuation value greater than 10 HU (99.5%)."

items.title: Why primary aldosteronism is systematically underdiagnosed
items.text: Despite affecting 5%–10% of hypertensive adults, primary aldosteronism is rarely considered or tested. Delayed diagnosis drives suboptimal quality of life and preventable cardiac and renal morbidity.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Even though primary aldosteronism affects 5% to 10% of all persons with hypertension, clinicians do not think about it very much and do not test for it."

items.title: How to perform case-detection testing for primary aldosteronism
items.text: Use a morning venipuncture for plasma aldosterone concentration and plasma renin activity or plasma renin concentration. Testing can be done without stopping most antihypertensive medications.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "Case-detection testing is performed with a morning venipuncture for the measurement of plasma aldosterone concentration and plasma renin activity or plasma renin concentration."

items.title: Why normal serum potassium does not exclude primary aldosteronism
items.text: Hypokalemia is present in only 28% of patients with primary aldosteronism; absence of hypokalemia is meaningless for case detection. All hypertensive patients are potential candidates.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "Because hypokalemia is present in only 28% of patients with primary aldosteronism, all patients with hypertension are potential candidates for this disorder."

items.title: Why cardiovascular harm in primary aldosteronism exceeds blood pressure alone
items.text: Meta-analysis shows increased stroke, coronary disease, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and LVH versus essential hypertension—aldosterone-specific toxicity beyond hypertension.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "Thus, the cardiovascular toxicity in primary aldosteronism extends beyond hypertension; there is an aldosterone-specific toxicity."

items.title: How to approach germline testing in pheochromocytoma
items.text: Genetic testing should be considered in all patients, especially with paraganglioma, bilateral disease, positive family history, age <60 years, or syndromic features. Counseling is recommended because results affect relatives.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "Genetic testing should be considered in all patients with pheochromocytoma, especially if a patient has 1 or more of the following: paraganglioma; bilateral adrenal pheochromocytoma"

items.title: When to consider genetic testing for familial primary aldosteronism
items.text: Consider familial forms when PA is diagnosed before age 20 or in multiple family members. Nearly all familial cases have bilateral adrenal disease and would not be cured by unilateral adrenalectomy.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "Familial primary aldosteronism should be considered when primary aldosteronism is diagnosed before age 20 years or when primary aldosteronism is diagnosed in more than one family member."

items.title: Changing patterns of pheochromocytoma discovery
items.text: In a recent Mayo series (2005–2016), 61% were adrenal incidentalomas and 12% detected via genetic/family testing—contrasting with older series where paroxysmal symptoms drove 90% of diagnoses.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "In the most recent series of patients with pheochromocytoma reported from Mayo Clinic (271 patients from 2005 to 2016), 61% were detected as adrenal incidentalomas and 12% were detected because of genetic or family testing."

items.title: How medications interfere with catecholamine testing
items.text: Tricyclic antidepressants (including cyclobenzaprine) and many psychoactive agents interfere most with urinary fractionated catecholamines/metanephrines—taper ≥4 weeks when possible. Antihypertensives and acetaminophen do not interfere with modern assays.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) interfere most frequently with the interpretation of 24-hour urinary fractionated catecholamines and metanephrines."

items.title: Why antihypertensives need not be stopped before primary aldosteronism screening
items.text: Patients can remain on any blood pressure medication—including spironolactone and eplerenone—during case-detection testing. Endocrinologists have created an unnecessary barrier by overemphasizing medication washout.
items.reference: Case 5: "Patients can be on any sodium diet and any blood pressure medication, including spironolactone and eplerenone, when performing case-detection testing for primary aldosteronism."

items.title: Why a synonymous VHL variant caused familial pheochromocytoma
items.text: A heterozygous synonymous VHL c.414A>G (p.Pro138Pro) promoted exon 2 skipping despite no amino-acid change—illustrating that 'silent' variants are not always neutral and can require research-grade sequencing.
items.reference: The Genetic Mystery Solved: "the c.414A>G VHL variant was found to promote exon 2 skipping."

items.question: Two 50-year-old women have nearly identical 2.8-cm right adrenal incidentalomas. Patient 1 has unenhanced attenuation 1.2 HU; Patient 2 has attenuation consistent with >10 HU. Who should be screened biochemically for pheochromocytoma?
items.explanation: Attenuation <10 HU effectively excludes pheochromocytoma; biochemical testing is unnecessary for Patient 1 and indicated for Patient 2.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: B) Patient 2"

items.question: A 49-year-old man has a lipid-poor, vascular 1.6-cm left adrenal mass (40.5 HU) with normal plasma and 24-hour urine metanephrines/catecholamines. Do normal biochemistry results exclude pheochromocytoma?
options: Yes — normal metanephrines exclude the diagnosis
No — small tumors may be prebiochemical
Only if repeat testing is normal in 6 months
items.explanation: Laparoscopic resection proved pheochromocytoma; small lipid-poor vascular masses can secrete below detection until they exceed ~1.5 cm.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: B) No"

items.question: A 49-year-old man (Case 2) undergoes adrenalectomy for pheochromocytoma without syndromic stigmata. Best next step for germline evaluation?
options: Next-generation sequencing panel for pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma susceptibility genes
No genetic testing — sporadic unilateral disease
items.explanation: Guidelines recommend genetic testing in all pheochromocytoma patients; a multigene NGS panel is the contemporary first-line approach in unselected cases.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "Genetic testing should be considered in all patients with pheochromocytoma, especially if a patient has 1 or more of the following"

items.question: A 23-year-old woman has new-onset hypertension without hypokalemia. A 35-year-old man has resistant hypertension on three agents, also normokalemic. Who should undergo primary aldosteronism case-detection testing?
options: Only the man with resistant hypertension
Only the woman because of young age
items.explanation: 70% of primary aldosteronism patients are normokalemic; missing PA in a 23-year-old with new hypertension would be a major diagnostic error.
items.reference: Case 4: "The lack of hypokalemia is meaningless—70% of patients with primary aldosteronism have a normal serum potassium concentration. Thus, both patients should be tested (Answer C)."

items.question: A 56-year-old man on lisinopril, spironolactone, HCTZ, metoprolol, and amlodipine (BP 165/110 mm Hg) is screened for primary aldosteronism. Which medication(s) must be stopped first?
options: All antihypertensives — switch to doxazosin and verapamil
None — testing can proceed on current medications
items.explanation: Case-detection can be performed on any antihypertensive regimen including mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists; stopping drugs creates an artificial barrier.
items.reference: Case 5: "Answer: F) None of the above"

items.question: A 56-year-old man with primary aldosteronism due to a unilateral aldosterone-producing adenoma is cured by adrenalectomy. Should he have genetic testing for familial hyperaldosteronism?
options: Yes — all PA patients need genetic testing
No — sporadic unilateral APA cured by surgery
items.explanation: Familial PA is rare and should be considered when diagnosed before age 20 or in multiple family members; bilateral disease is typical and unilateral adrenalectomy would not cure familial forms.
items.reference: Case 6: "Answer: B) No"

items.question: Which clinical scenario should raise suspicion for pheochromocytoma?
options: Pressor crisis during anesthesia in a patient with adrenal incidentaloma
Isolated systolic hypertension with wide pulse pressure
Mild orthostatic hypotension in an elderly patient
items.explanation: Pressor response to anesthesia, surgery, angiography, high-dose corticosteroid, or beta-blocker is a classic pheochromocytoma trigger alongside incidentaloma and familial syndromes.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "A pressor response to anesthesia, surgery, angiography, high-dose corticosteroid, or β-adrenergic blocker"

items.question: At Mayo Clinic, the preferred initial case-detection strategy for pheochromocytoma is:
options: Plasma fractionated metanephrines alone in all patients
items.explanation: 24-hour urine fractionated metanephrines and catecholamines is the most reliable case-detection strategy; plasma metanephrines are added when clinical suspicion is high.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "At Mayo Clinic, the most reliable case-detection strategy is measuring fractionated metanephrines and catecholamines in a 24-hour urine collection."

items.question: A 32-year-old has marked hypokalemia but BP 132/82 mm Hg (baseline ~100/60). What is the best interpretation?
options: Hypokalemia cannot be primary aldosteronism without hypertension
Clinically significant BP rise from baseline may represent early primary aldosteronism
items.explanation: Young patients may not meet formal hypertension criteria early in disease because counter-regulatory mechanisms and low baseline pressures mask the aldosterone effect.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "Thus, although they do not meet the criteria for hypertension, there is a clinically significant change from baseline"

items.question: An asymptomatic adult with family history of pheochromocytoma asks for predictive genetic testing. When is testing appropriate?
options: Only if an affected relative has a known pathogenic variant
After a positive plasma metanephrine test
items.explanation: Predictive testing in at-risk relatives requires a known familial pathogenic variant from an affected proband; counseling coordinates cascade testing.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "An asymptomatic person at risk for disease on the basis of family history of pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma should have genetic testing only if an affected family member has a known pathogenic variant."

items.question: A postpartum woman develops Takotsubo-pattern cardiomyopathy, elevated troponin, and markedly elevated urinary normetanephrine with a 4.5-cm right adrenal mass. Family history reveals multiple pheochromocytomas. Initial management priority?
options: Alpha-blockade before beta-blockade and planned adrenalectomy
items.explanation: Pheochromocytoma can mimic acute coronary syndromes; alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) precedes beta-blockade, with definitive resection after biochemical control.
items.reference: Case 7: "Treatment with phenoxybenzamine is initiated, and CT of the abdomen detects a 4.5-cm right adrenal mass."

items.question: Compared with essential hypertension, primary aldosteronism is associated with which outcome?
options: Lower stroke risk due to younger diagnosis
Increased atrial fibrillation risk (OR ~3.5 in meta-analysis)
Reduced LVH due to earlier mineralocorticoid antagonism
items.explanation: Meta-analysis of 31 studies showed higher stroke, CAD, AF, heart failure, diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and LVH versus essential hypertension.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "patients with aldosterone-producing adenomas and bilateral hyperplasia had an increased risk of stroke (odds ratio [OR], 2.58), coronary artery disease (OR, 1.77), atrial fibrillation (OR, 3.52), and heart failure (OR, 2.05)."

items.question: In adults under 40 years with hypertension, approximately what proportion has secondary hypertension?
items.explanation: Secondary hypertension affects >50% of children and ~30% of young adults (<40 years), making targeted endocrine screening especially important in this age group.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "More than 50% of children and 30% of young adults (<40 years) have secondary hypertension."

items.statement: An adrenal incidentaloma with unenhanced CT attenuation less than 10 HU can still be a pheochromocytoma.
items.explanation: 99.5% of confirmed pheochromocytomas have attenuation >10 HU; values below this threshold effectively exclude the diagnosis.
items.reference: Case 1: "An adrenal incidentaloma with an unenhanced CT attenuation of 1.2 HU simply cannot be a pheochromocytoma."

items.statement: Normal plasma and urine metanephrines always exclude a small pheochromocytoma.
items.explanation: Small lipid-poor vascular adrenal masses can be prebiochemical until they exceed roughly 1.5 cm in diameter.
items.reference: Case 2: "all pheochromocytomas are “prebiochemical” when small."

items.statement: Hypokalemia is present in most patients with primary aldosteronism.
items.explanation: Only 28% have hypokalemia; normokalemia does not exclude the diagnosis.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "Because hypokalemia is present in only 28% of patients with primary aldosteronism"

items.statement: Spironolactone must be discontinued before primary aldosteronism case-detection testing.
items.explanation: Patients can be tested while on spironolactone, eplerenone, or any antihypertensive regimen.
items.reference: Case 5: "Patients can be on any sodium diet and any blood pressure medication, including spironolactone and eplerenone, when performing case-detection testing for primary aldosteronism."

items.statement: Genetic testing should be considered in all patients diagnosed with pheochromocytoma.
items.explanation: Especially with paraganglioma, bilateral disease, family history, age <60, or syndromic features—but consideration applies broadly.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "Genetic testing should be considered in all patients with pheochromocytoma"

items.statement: A patient cured of primary aldosteronism by unilateral adrenalectomy at age 56 should routinely receive familial hyperaldosteronism genetic testing.
items.explanation: Familial PA testing is indicated when diagnosed before age 20 or in multiple family members; sporadic unilateral APA does not warrant routine genetic testing.
items.reference: Case 6: "Thus, there is no role for genetic testing in the patient described this clinical vignette."

items.statement: With modern HPLC/tandem mass spectrometry assays, antihypertensive medications interfere with plasma metanephrine testing.
items.explanation: Antihypertensive medications and acetaminophen do not interfere with current assay methodology.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "with current assay methodology (tandem mass spectrometry, high-performance liquid chromatography), antihypertensive medications and acetaminophen do not interfere with testing."

items.statement: Primary aldosteronism affects approximately 5% to 10% of all patients with hypertension.
items.explanation: Current prevalence estimates support screening a broad hypertensive population, not only hypokalemic or resistant cases.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "Current prevalence estimates for primary aldosteronism are 5% to 10% of all patients with hypertension."

items.assertion: Assertion: Adrenal incidentalomas with unenhanced attenuation below 10 HU do not require biochemical pheochromocytoma screening.
items.reason: Reason: More than 99% of histologically confirmed pheochromocytomas have unenhanced attenuation greater than 10 HU.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and causally linked—low HU reliably excludes pheochromocytoma.
items.reference: Case 1: "374 had an attenuation value greater than 10 HU (99.5%)."

items.assertion: Assertion: A lipid-poor vascular adrenal mass can harbor pheochromocytoma despite normal metanephrines.
items.reason: Reason: Pheochromocytomas may be prebiochemical until they reach approximately 1.5 cm diameter.
items.explanation: Both are true; small tumors secrete below biochemical detection thresholds.
items.reference: Case 2: "Pheochromocytomas need a “big factory” (typically >1.5 cm in diameter) to be biochemically detectable."

items.assertion: Assertion: Primary aldosteronism is frequently missed in clinical practice.
items.reason: Reason: Clinicians rarely test for a disorder they perceive as uncommon despite 5%–10% prevalence.
items.explanation: Both true; low testing rates despite substantial population prevalence drive underdiagnosis.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "clinicians do not think about it very much and do not test for it."

items.reason: Reason: Most patients with primary aldosteronism have hypokalemia at presentation.
items.explanation: A is false (only 28% are hypokalemic); R is false (most are normokalemic).
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "Because hypokalemia is present in only 28% of patients with primary aldosteronism"

items.assertion: Assertion: Primary aldosteronism confers cardiovascular risk beyond that of blood pressure elevation alone.
items.reason: Reason: Aldosterone has direct target-organ toxicity independent of hypertension.
items.explanation: Both true; meta-analysis demonstrates aldosterone-specific toxicity.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "there is an aldosterone-specific toxicity."

items.assertion: Assertion: Familial primary aldosteronism is usually cured by unilateral adrenalectomy.
items.reason: Reason: Nearly all familial cases have bilateral adrenal disease.
items.explanation: Both false—familial PA is typically bilateral and not cured by unilateral surgery.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism: "In nearly all cases, patients with familial primary aldosteronism have bilateral adrenal disease and would not be cured with unilateral adrenalectomy."

items.assertion: Assertion: Tricyclic antidepressants can cause false-positive urinary catecholamine testing.
items.reason: Reason: TCAs should be tapered and discontinued at least 4 weeks before testing when clinically feasible.
items.explanation: Both true; TCAs are the most common biochemical interferent.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "Treatment with TCAs and antipsychotic agents should be tapered and discontinued at least 4 weeks before testing."

items.assertion: Assertion: Asymptomatic relatives should undergo genetic testing for pheochromocytoma predisposition.
items.reason: Reason: Testing should proceed only when an affected family member has a known pathogenic variant.
items.explanation: Both true but R does not explain A—predictive testing requires a known familial variant first.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma: "should have genetic testing only if an affected family member has a known pathogenic variant."
